_note: Build source for the personal year card feature. Mirrors content/year-card-reading.md (the human-readable review copy). index 0-21 matches MAJOR_SLUGS/MAJORS in lib/almanac.ts. blurb = free-tier year-card meaning; whatToDo = the per-page 'what to do with this year' line. Keep in sync with the markdown.

cards.blurb: A Fool year starts you over. Something opens up that you don't have the map for yet, and the invitation is to step toward it before you can see where your foot lands. Expect first days more than finished things: the job you're not qualified for on paper, the city you move to knowing no one, the practice you pick up decades later than the world says you should. You carry less than you think you need and more faith than you're used to trusting. The year asks you to be a beginner on purpose.
cards.whatToDo: Start the thing before you feel ready, and let being a beginner be part of it. When the urge to leap shows up, check once whether you're moving toward something or away from it, then go.

cards.blurb: This is the year the tools turn out to be already in your hands. A Magician year is for making something real: you stop researching how and open the document, you have the conversation you already know the words for. Scattered energy becomes a single clear aim. Whatever you'd filed under someday or out of reach is usually built from what's already on your desk, and the year keeps handing you the moment to pick it up and point it somewhere. You are more resourced than you've been treating yourself as.
cards.whatToDo: Name the one thing you actually want this year, out loud, then take inventory of what's already on your desk to build it with. When the impostor story starts, set it down and pick up a tool instead.

cards.blurb: A High Priestess year turns the volume down so you can hear yourself. The knowing arrives before the reasons do: the read on a person in the first ten seconds, the decision you wake up having already made. This is a year to trust the signal you can't yet show your work for, and to protect an inner life from the pressure to explain it. Less building, more listening. Some of your sharpest intelligence this year never bothers to put itself into words, and it's right anyway.
cards.whatToDo: Make room for real quiet this year, the kind where you can hear your own read on things. When a knowing arrives that you can't defend yet, try acting on it once and see, instead of talking yourself out of it.

cards.blurb: Things grow in an Empress year, and you're allowed to enjoy that they do. Ideas come faster than you can write them down. You make things and let yourself want them without the running tally of whether you earned it. This is a year for tending: your own ground first, then whatever and whoever you're growing. It runs through the body and the senses, through good food and real rest and being cared for instead of only doing the caring. Creation here isn't forced. You give life room and it takes it.
cards.whatToDo: Tend your own ground before you pour into anyone else's. Let yourself receive care this year, not only give it, and make something for the pleasure of it rather than to prove you earned the rest.

cards.blurb: An Emperor year is for building something solid enough to stand on. You set the deadline and mean it. You hold the boundary without three paragraphs of apology after. This is the year you become the steady point other people organize themselves around, the calm one when something goes wrong. Structure here is a form of care: it gives your vision a body and turns what you meant to do into something that lasts. You're allowed to lead this year. You're allowed to be the one who decides.
cards.whatToDo: Build the structure that serves what you actually care about: the routine, the boundary, the plan you'll keep. Say the hard no without the apology paragraph, and let people help hold it up.

cards.blurb: You don't have to start from nothing this year. A Hierophant year puts teachers, traditions, and rooms with a history in your path: the mentor who shortens your road by years, the practice that has steadied people long before you found it. You inherit what resonates and leave the rest. There's real relief in belonging somewhere older than this moment, in doing work you didn't have to invent alone. The year rewards apprenticeship, the willingness to learn inside a structure rather than reinvent every wheel yourself.
cards.whatToDo: Find the teacher, room, or tradition that fits your real values, and learn inside it this year. Keep what resonates, question what doesn't without throwing the whole thing out, and notice what you inherited versus what you'd choose.

cards.blurb: A Lovers year asks your whole self to show up for the choice. It's the year of being known and not turned away: telling someone the thing you were sure would make them leave, and watching them move closer. The big choices land in the body. The ones that fit settle you; the ones that don't keep you slightly clenched no matter how good they look on paper. Expect to choose a person, a path, a place, and to feel whether you actually agree with the choice. What you want and what you value either point the same way this year, or you find out where they don't.
cards.whatToDo: When a big choice comes, check it against your values rather than your fears, and notice how it sits in your body. Name a need before it hardens into resentment, and let yourself be seen a little past comfortable.

cards.blurb: The Chariot year is when the wanting becomes moving. The project that kept circling finally has motion under it. The decision gets made and acted on the same week instead of deliberated for a year. Drive doesn't have to look like anyone else's to be real: it can be slow, soft, nonlinear, and still carry you where you meant to go. The work of the year is holding your own reins, keeping the pulling forces pointed one direction by your own focus. You know what moves you. This year you get to use it.
cards.whatToDo: Point your drive at something you chose, then let it move even when progress is slow or uneven. Before a big push, check what's actually driving it, and give yourself permission to reroute when the road stops being yours.

cards.blurb: A Strength year hands you your own intensity to hold. That's the work of it: staying steady in the hard conversation instead of going cold or going off, feeling the fear all the way through and doing the thing anyway. Your calm this year isn't compliance and your gentleness isn't weakness. The power on offer is quiet, the kind that meets its own anger and grief and desire with a hand on the jaw rather than a fist. You don't have to roar to get through this year. You have to stay.
cards.whatToDo: When intensity rises this year, breathe through it instead of bracing, and name the feeling before it names you. Meet your hardest moments with some compassion, and let yourself be helped rather than proving you can hold it all alone.

cards.blurb: A Hermit year turns you inward to hear what's true. The clarity comes once you get quiet: the walk alone where the knot you couldn't untangle in a loud room comes clear, the evening by yourself you wake up steadier for. This is a year to turn the world down far enough to hear your own read on your own life. You carry the light and don't have to borrow it. Solitude isn't loneliness this year. It's where you go to come back to yourself, and the answers you find there are the ones you can trust.
cards.whatToDo: Protect real solitude this year, and use it to listen for your own counsel before borrowing anyone else's. Watch the line between reflection and avoidance, and come back to the world when you're ready, not when you're called.

cards.blurb: The Wheel year turns whether or not you're ready. Change arrives that you didn't schedule: the chance out of nowhere, the door that opens the week you'd given up knocking, timing you could never have engineered. Some of it lifts you and some of it drops you, and the year's real practice is trusting motion you can't fully control. Your life doesn't have to run in a straight line to be going somewhere. Learning to move with the turn instead of bracing against it is where the year gets lighter.
cards.whatToDo: Track your own seasons this year without judging the low ones, and notice the small turns, not just the dramatic ones. Sort what's yours to steer from what isn't, and put your energy into the first.

cards.blurb: A Justice year wants the truth from you, even when it costs something. It's the year of the honest look: your actual part in the mess instead of the case for your innocence, the conversation where you stop spinning it and say what happened. Something unclenches when you do. Integrity is the through-line, the match between what you do and what you know is right, and this year keeps testing it. You don't owe anyone perfection. You can name harm where it lives, meet what's real, and be answerable without looking away.
cards.whatToDo: Tell the truth this year, even the expensive kind, and try writing it before you say it out loud. Look honestly at your own part without spiraling into shame, and name what real repair would actually take.

cards.blurb: The Hanged One year finds its wisdom in not acting. Some knot solves itself the moment you stop forcing it. Some answer arrives on the walk where you'd given up thinking about it. This is a year where waiting can be wiser than any rushed choice, where choosing not to decide today changes what you can see. Hanging still, you catch the thing from an angle effort could never reach. The pause stops feeling like failure. It starts to feel like the part of the year that was doing the actual work.
cards.whatToDo: Let a stuck decision wait this year without calling it avoidance, and look at the thing from a genuinely new angle. Practice real stillness rather than frozen worry, and notice when the pause has done its work and it's time to move.

cards.blurb: A Death year ends what you've outgrown so something else can begin. It's the year you leave the job, the relationship, the version of yourself you kept wearing because it was familiar. The grief finally moves once you stop pretending the thing wasn't already over. Endings here are thresholds, not punishments. When you let the dead thing be dead, you stop pouring energy into keeping it upright, and you get that energy back for whatever is trying to start. Expect to make room. The year is clearing ground on purpose.
cards.whatToDo: Name what's actually over this year, out loud, and let the grief move instead of managing it. Where you can choose the ending, choose it, and support your body through the change rather than pushing past it.

cards.blurb: A Temperance year blends what doesn't obviously go together. You love someone and stay angry at them in the same breath, and let both be true instead of picking one. The recovery holds because you stopped white-knuckling it and let it take its time. This is slow alchemy, a year that works by mixing rather than perfecting, and it rewards patience practiced as an actual skill. You can hold two things that seem to contradict and find they both live in you. Healing this year doesn't have to look like anyone else's, or be finished, to be real.
cards.whatToDo: Practice patience as an actual skill this year, not a personality trait. Hold two true things at once without forcing them to resolve early, and pace yourself before the burnout rather than after.

cards.blurb: The Devil year asks you to look straight at what you can't stop wanting. There's a strange relief in it: saying out loud that you check his location when he's late, that the second glass is the one you wait all day for, that you stayed for the status and not the work. Naming the craving shows you how much energy it took to keep it hidden. A want you can look at directly loses most of its power over you. Your appetite isn't shameful this year. Seeing the chain clearly is the first move toward noticing it was never locked.
cards.whatToDo: Name the want you've been talking around this year, somewhere it can be witnessed. Ask what the craving is actually trying to get you, and test whether the chain is locked or just heavy enough that you stopped pulling.

cards.blurb: A Tower year brings down what was never going to hold. The job that ends in a day, the truth that comes out and can't go back in, the story you'd been holding up with both hands that finally drops. It hurts, and it clears. What's false goes so suddenly there's no putting it back, and the air afterward is strangely clean. You're not breaking this year. You're breaking free of a structure that wasn't built to hold you. Let it fall. You never needed that particular cage as much as you feared.
cards.whatToDo: When something falls this year, ground your body first, then name what was false or forced in the thing that broke. Give the grief a container, and rebuild slowly without rushing to look fine.

cards.blurb: A Star year is when you notice you want things again. The appetite comes back after grief. You catch yourself making a plan for a future you'd stopped believing in. Beauty lands for the first time in a long while. This is gentle and real, hope that came through the hard year before it and made it out. Your healing counts this year even when it's slow. You're allowed to believe in a future that looks nothing like the blueprint you were handed. The light came back, and you don't have to apologize for feeling it.
cards.whatToDo: Let hope be small and tentative this year, and notice beauty again on purpose. Reconnect through water, nature, or touch, and heal on your own timeline instead of for an audience.

cards.blurb: A Moon year has you moving through what you can't fully see. The knowing comes without daylight: the dream that says the thing your waking mind refused, the bad feeling about the offer that looks perfect on paper, the ease with a stranger you can't yet explain. This is a year to feel your way and trust yourself anyway. Your truth is allowed to be fluid, your read valid even when it makes no logical sense. Some passages only cross by feel. Moving that way this year isn't the same as being lost.
cards.whatToDo: Let a dream or a gut-pull tell you something this year, and tell intuition apart from anxiety by how it sits in your body. When the map runs out, move by feel, and stay with the questions that don't have answers yet.

cards.blurb: A Sun year lets you be glad without bracing for it to end. You dance in the kitchen because a song came on. You tell the story at dinner without scanning their faces to see if it lands. The warmth of being fully yourself in front of people turns out to be safe. This is the small everyday magic you actually let yourself enjoy, confidence that isn't performed because it's just how you feel. You deserve to be seen this year. After a long dark, you get to be glad, out loud, in daylight.
cards.whatToDo: Let yourself enjoy a good thing this year without immediately bracing for it to end. Celebrate the small wins instead of rushing past them, and let yourself be visible where it's safe.

cards.blurb: A Judgement year is the one where you stop arguing the old case against yourself. You look back at a version of you you've been ashamed of and feel something closer to tenderness than the usual wince. You're more than the worst things you did and the worst things done to you, and this year you get to set the file down. Something is calling you to become someone new, and you don't need permission to answer it. When the change gets noticed, you can take it in instead of deflecting it back into the hole you climbed out of.
cards.whatToDo: Forgive an older version of yourself this year, on purpose, and review your life without putting yourself on trial. Take in growth and praise without deflecting it, and let yourself rise before you feel fully ready.

cards.blurb: A World year is arriving, whole, at the end of something real. The final draft submitted and the laptop closed. The last box unpacked in a home that's actually yours. You look around a life you built and recognize it. The wholeness here isn't something you have to keep earning, the cycle closed cleanly enough that you can feel it. Celebrate the becoming this year. And underneath the celebration, notice the quiet readiness: a completion is also a doorway, and you get to begin again whenever you are ready.
cards.whatToDo: Let yourself actually feel a completion this year instead of rushing to the next thing. Name what you learned and lived, close the cycle with some ritual, and hold that the ending is also where the next thing begins.

faq.a: It's the one Major Arcana card set for you for a given calendar year, found from your birthday through tarot numerology. Everyone has one. It's the same card every time you check for that year, and it changes from year to year. Think of it as the overarching theme the year is working on with you, the weather the twelve months move through.

Add your birth month, your birth day, and the digits of the year, then wrap the total onto the 22 Major Arcana. Born March 15, looking at 2026: 3 + 15 + (2 + 0 + 2 + 6) = 28, and 28 wraps around to 6, The Lovers. Nothing is shuffled. The same birthday and year always land on the same card.

faq.q: Why is knowing your year card useful?
faq.a: It gives the year a throughline. Instead of facing twelve months as one undifferentiated stretch, you have a lens: a single theme to notice, work with, and check your choices against. People use it the way they'd use a word of the year, except this one comes with a whole card's worth of meaning and a reason behind it.

faq.q: What do I actually do with it?
faq.a: Read it at the start of the year and let it set an intention or two. Come back to it when a decision comes up and ask how this year's theme would meet it. Some people revisit it at the start of each month, or journal the reflection questions from the full reading. It works best as something you return to, not something you read once.

No. The card doesn't decide what happens to you. It offers an angle on the year and some questions to think through, and what you do with that is yours. It can influence you if you let it, which is different from fate.

faq.q: How is this different from a normal tarot reading?
faq.a: A normal reading is drawn: you shuffle, and chance sets the cards. This one is given. Your birthday and the year fix the card through numerology, so it's the same every time and you're not hoping for a lucky pull. It reflects the pattern your dates already make.

faq.q: Why is my card different from one I got somewhere else?
faq.a: Most tarot year card calculators reduce your numbers all the way down, which can only ever reach part of the Major Arcana. The Almanac keeps the whole wheel of twenty-two and wraps around instead of reducing, so it can land on any card, including the ones a reduced method never reaches. If another calculator gave you a different card, that's the reason.

faq.q: Does the card change every year? What about the months?
faq.a: Yes, it changes from year to year. And within a year, each of the twelve months has its own card, stepping forward one place at a time from your year card, so the year reads as a path rather than a single note. The full year-ahead reading walks all twelve, with the woven narrative and your reflection questions.
